critic_raw:
This experiment tests an ensemble of XGBoost on structured features and logistic regression on clinical notes TF-IDF against a logistic regression baseline on structured features only.

The ensemble includes clinical notes as an input modality; the baseline does not. This is the same fatal comparison design flaw as in other cases here: a modality difference is conflated with a methodology difference. The hypothesis is about the ensemble approach, but the test will be confounded by the fact that clinical notes provide additional information the baseline cannot access. The only valid control would include a baseline that also uses notes (e.g., logistic regression on structured features plus TF-IDF features concatenated), which is exactly Model B in isolation. Since Model B already exists in the design, reporting its standalone performance against the baseline would decompose the effect cleanly, but this comparison is not described.

The clinical notes TF-IDF vectorization uses the top 500 features. This is an arbitrary cutoff that could strongly influence the result: 500 is small enough that the TF-IDF component may capture only the most common terms in notes, potentially including demographic or procedural boilerplate rather than clinically informative content. At this scale, TF-IDF may merely replicate information already in the structured features rather than genuinely adding new signal. No ablation or sensitivity analysis on the vocabulary size is described.

The ensemble weights are tuned on the validation set. This is an additional model selection step that uses the validation set beyond the hyperparameter searches for Model A and Model B individually. With small validation sets (~10,000 records, but only ~15–20% positive), the variance in AUROC estimates is substantial enough that optimal ensemble weights may be overfitted to validation noise. No confidence interval on the ensemble weighting procedure is reported.

The TF-IDF vectorizer vocabulary is fit on training data, which is correct. However, if admission, progress, and discharge notes have different term distributions — and particularly if the notes available at prediction time (discharge) differ from those available at admission — the model conflates information available at different clinical timepoints. Using discharge summaries as a feature for predicting readmission requires verifying that discharge summary content is not influenced by the fact of readmission (e.g., clinicians writing more detailed notes for complex patients who they know are at risk).
all_issues_raised: The ensemble uses clinical notes as an additional modality not available to the baseline. The performance comparison conflates ensemble methodology with information access — a single-task model using both structured features and notes is the missing control.

The TF-IDF vocabulary is capped at 500 features, which is small enough to primarily capture administrative boilerplate rather than clinically distinctive content. No sensitivity analysis on vocabulary size is described.

Ensemble weights tuned on a validation set of ~10,000 records with 15–20% positive rate will have substantial variance in AUROC estimates, making weight optimization susceptible to overfitting to validation noise with no confidence interval reported.

Discharge summaries used as a feature may contain content influenced by clinician foreknowledge of readmission risk, introducing a form of label leakage if clinicians document more thoroughly for patients they perceive as high-risk.
all_issues_adjudicated: The ensemble uses clinical notes as an additional modality not available to the baseline. The performance comparison conflates ensemble methodology with information access — a single-task model using both structured features and notes is the missing control.

The TF-IDF vocabulary is capped at 500 features, which is small enough to primarily capture administrative boilerplate rather than clinically distinctive content. No sensitivity analysis on vocabulary size is described.

Ensemble weights tuned on a validation set of ~10,000 records with 15–20% positive rate will have substantial variance in AUROC estimates, making weight optimization susceptible to overfitting to validation noise with no confidence interval reported.

Discharge summaries used as a feature may contain content influenced by clinician foreknowledge of readmission risk, introducing a form of label leakage if clinicians document more thoroughly for patients they perceive as high-risk.
